steps.title: Pin down the anachronism
steps.content: Titanium dioxide entered ink manufacturing in the early 1900s. If the Vinland Map's ink contains it, the map cannot predate that invention — so a genuine 1400s artifact is ruled out.

steps.title: Match the conclusion to each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — 'mid-1400s Europeans could not have known about the eastern coast of present-day North America.': About geographic knowledge, not about ink chemistry — the passage never addresses this. ✗
  • Choice B — 'the Vinland Map could not have been drawn by mid-1400s mapmakers.': Follows directly: an ingredient that didn't exist until the 1900s cannot appear in a 1400s map, so the map must be a forgery. ✓
  • Choice C — 'mapmakers must have used titanium compounds in their ink in the 1400s.': Contradicts the passage, which says titanium dioxide only appeared in ink in the early 1900s. ✗
  • Choice D — 'there isn't enough information to determine when the ink was created.': Contradicts the finding — the presence of a post-1900 compound is precisely the information needed to date the ink. ✗
That matches choice B — B is correct.
